metric_value: Siebel Systems has over 214 employees
Three elements driving rapid growth: robust N-tiered architecture; partner-leveraged business model; rapid application development methodology
notes: Aberdeen's three-factor growth model for Siebel

metric_value: Aberdeen expects 1997 will be another successful year for Siebel; company positioned as major and rapidly growing force in CIS market
Siebel 1997 revenue rose to $120 million
Siebel 1998 revenue rose 89% to $391.5 million; net income rose 135% to $55.7 million
Siebel revenue surpassed $1 billion mark in 2000; named fastest-growing US company by Fortune magazine in 1999
Oracle acquired Siebel Systems January 2006 for $5.85 billion; Siebel achieved peak CRM market share of 45% circa 2002
Siebel Enterprise Applications built on N-tiered client-server architecture; supports Windows and Windows NT clients; Unix and Windows NT servers; Oracle, Sybase, Informix and MS SQL Server databases
Siebel Enterprise Applications written in Microsoft Visual C++ and MFC; object-oriented design
Siebel announced Version 3.0 in January 1997 with enhanced customization via Siebel Tools; remote software distribution; Application Upgrader; CTI integration
Siebel anticipated need for pen-based computing as example of GUI advantage and field sales use case
notes: Early mobile/pen computing prediction for CRM

metric_value: Pen computing prediction proved prescient: mobile CRM on smartphones and tablets became standard by 2010s; Siebel Mobile was released but Salesforce.com led cloud/mobile CRM
notes: Directionally correct; specific product leadership went to Salesforce

metric_value: Siebel uses a virtual professional services organization through partners (Andersen Consulting, KPMG, Cambridge Technology Partners, Price Waterhouse) rather than building its own services capacity
In March 1997, Siebel and Compaq announced alliance under which they mutually recognize each other as preferred provider in CIS market
Siebel's Microsoft alliance is described as most significant; Siebel provides full support for MS Application Development Tools, MS Office and MS BackOffice
Siebel Tools allows graphical configuration of application to unique requirements without altering source code; modular N-tiered architecture using configurable business objects (BusObjects)
Siebel's N-tiered architecture allows flexible deployment: personal computing (client-centric); client-server; or virtual computing (network/server-centric) using Internet-based technologies
Siebel's solutions appropriate for firms selling directly or through indirect channels; ideal for large multi-national firms with multiple distribution channels
Siebel 3.0 integrates with CTI middleware from Prospect and Genesys; supports automatic call distributors from Aspect, AT&T and Nortel
Aberdeen expects Siebel-Microsoft relationship will increase in value for both companies throughout 1997
Siebel-Microsoft relationship remained important through late 1990s; but Microsoft eventually developed its own CRM (Microsoft Dynamics CRM in 2003); relationship became competitive
notes: Microsoft entered CRM market directly competing with Siebel

metric_value: Siebel Application Upgrader is unique: enables new software releases to be seamlessly merged with previous versions and user customizations by a single command
Aberdeen expects Siebel to capitalize on ability to help forward-looking organizations increase revenues while driving down costs in growing CIS market
CRM market projected to reach $16.8 billion by 2003; Siebel dominated with ~45% market share at peak circa 2002
Siebel's SFA competitors named as Vantive Corp., Aurum Corp. (Baan subsidiary), and Clarify Corp.
